node.title: Uber | SDE-1 | Interview Exp | CTC 65L | Game Theory + Multi-dimensional DP

article.title: Uber | SDE-1 | Interview Exp | CTC 65L | Game Theory + Multi-dimensional DP
article.content:
Was asked this DSA Question - 
At a team event, engineers at Uber are sitting around a circular table playing a modified Rock–Paper–Scissors game.
Each engineer secretly chooses one of:
'R' → Rock
'P' → Paper
'S' → Scissors
After revealing their choices, every engineer compares their choice with their two immediate neighbors.
Two neighbors tie if they choose the same option.
Uber wants to make the game more interesting by ensuring no ties occur between any pair of neighbors.
You are allowed to ask any engineer to change their choice to either of the other two options.
Your task is to compute the minimum number of engineers whose choice must be changed so that no two adjacent engineers choose the same option.

Note that the engineers sit in a circle, so the first and last engineers are also neighbors.

Function Signature
int minChanges(string choices);
Input

A string choices of length n representing the selections of engineers sitting clockwise.

choices[i] ∈ {'R','P','S'}
Constraints
3 ≤ n ≤ 2 * 10^5

The arrangement is circular.

Example 1

Input

PRSSP

Output

2

Explanation

Two neighboring pairs have the same choice:

P R S S P
      ↑
      S S

and the first and last:

P .... P

Changing two engineers is enough to remove all ties.

Example 2

Input

RRRRRRR

Output

4
Example 3

Input

RSPRPSPRS

Output

0

No adjacent engineers choose the same option.

post.content:
In O(n) time, you can check if there are two in a row of the same, and if so, change the second one to the third option that doesn't collide with its previous or next option.

For your example:

PRSSP

Let's assume we start at index = 1 with zero indexing. We will check whether it matches the previous option. This means we don't trigger anything until the second S, and we change it to R as the previous value is S, and the next is P.

We will have to have a hard coded instance for the wrap around, and in case of my algorithm, we include this final check at the end, so we check the "second" P on this last check, and change it to S since the previous is P and the next is R.

You also do this in real time, so that if you have a long line of the same letter, you "skip" over every other letter.
